title: The Mole and Stoichiometry
subject: AP Chemistry
objectives: Define the mole and use Avogadro's number to convert between atoms/molecules and moles
Calculate molar mass from the periodic table and use it to convert between grams and moles
Solve stoichiometry problems using dimensional analysis and mole ratios from balanced equations

sections.heading: The Mole: Chemistry's Counting Unit
sections.content:
Atoms and molecules are far too small to count individually. Chemists use the mole as a counting unit, just as you use 'dozen' to mean 12. One mole of anything contains 6.022 × 10²³ particles — this is Avogadro's number.

Why that specific number? It was chosen so that one mole of carbon-12 atoms has a mass of exactly 12 grams. This creates a beautifully simple bridge: the atomic mass on the periodic table (in amu for one atom) equals the molar mass (in grams for one mole of atoms).

Carbon: atomic mass = 12.01 amu → molar mass = 12.01 g/mol
Oxygen: atomic mass = 16.00 amu → molar mass = 16.00 g/mol
Water (H₂O): molar mass = 2(1.008) + 16.00 = 18.02 g/mol

So 18.02 grams of water contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules. That is roughly one tablespoon of water.

sections.heading: Mass-to-Mass Stoichiometry via Dimensional Analysis
sections.content:
Problem: How many grams of CO₂ are produced when 10.0 g of CH₄ is burned?

Balanced equation: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O

Step 1: Convert grams of CH₄ to moles.
  10.0 g CH₄ × (1 mol CH₄ / 16.04 g) = 0.624 mol CH₄

Step 2: Use the mole ratio from the balanced equation.
  0.624 mol CH₄ × (1 mol CO₂ / 1 mol CH₄) = 0.624 mol CO₂

Step 3: Convert moles of CO₂ to grams.
  0.624 mol CO₂ × (44.01 g / 1 mol CO₂) = 27.5 g CO₂

The dimensional analysis chain:
  10.0 g CH₄ × (1 mol / 16.04 g) × (1 mol CO₂ / 1 mol CH₄) × (44.01 g / 1 mol) = 27.5 g CO₂

Every stoichiometry problem follows this pattern: grams → moles → mole ratio → moles → grams. The mole is always the bridge.

The mole bridges the atomic world (amu, individual particles) and the laboratory world (grams, measurable quantities). Every stoichiometry problem routes through moles: grams → moles → mole ratio → moles → grams.

sections.heading: Why Dimensional Analysis Is Your Best Friend
sections.content:
Dimensional analysis is not just a technique — it is a way of thinking that prevents errors. The idea is simple: set up your calculation as a chain of conversion factors so that unwanted units cancel and desired units remain.

Every conversion factor is a fraction equal to 1:
  (1 mol / 16.04 g CH₄) = 1 because 1 mol of CH₄ IS 16.04 g
  (6.022 × 10²³ molecules / 1 mol) = 1 because 1 mol IS 6.022 × 10²³ molecules

If you set up the chain and the units cancel properly, the math must be correct. If the units do not cancel, you know immediately that something is wrong — before you waste time calculating.

This approach works for any unit conversion in any science: grams to moles, moles to particles, liters to moles (using molarity), joules to calories, miles to kilometers. Master dimensional analysis now and you will use it for the rest of your science career.

aiContext: AP Chemistry. Use dimensional analysis for all calculations. Stress WHY reactions happen, not just equation balancing. Common confusion: entropy vs enthalpy direction, and applying Le Chatelier to specific perturbations.
